fund.name: WhiteOak Capital ELSS Tax Saver Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: Since you're 22 and haven't used your 80C deduction yet, this ELSS fund saves you up to ₹46,800 in tax right from the start (investment of ₹1.5 lakh gets you ₹5.4k credit in the new regime on your ₹1.5L investment, plus 3 years of growth). It also gives you equity exposure with a shorter 3-year lock-in. WhiteOak has a great track record in tax savers.

fund.name: Bank of India Mid & Small Cap Equity & Debt Fund - Direct Plan Growth

allocations.rationale: This is our engine for wealth creation. It mixes stocks with a bit of safety (debt) to keep things from getting too wild. Since you're conservative but have 38 years, this hybrid approach is perfect. It gives you growth without feeling too risky because it smooths out the ups and downs.

fund.name: Unifi Dynamic Asset Allocation Fund - Direct Growth

allocations.rationale: This fund automatically switches between stocks and safe assets based on market conditions. Think of it as a sleep well at night fund. You don't need to make decisions; the manager does. Since you're new to investing, this prevents you from panicking when the market drops. The manager has a fantastic track record.
Gold is like an insurance policy for your portfolio. When stocks suffer badly, gold usually helps. It's a small slice (15%) to protect your big gains..axis ETF is old and trusted.

plan.setup_phase: Step 1: Open a 'Direct' Mutual Fund account on an app like Groww, Coin, or Kuvera. In the app, select 'Direct Plan' – this is cheaper than 'Regular Plan' and keeps more of your money. Step 2: For tax saving, enter your bank account details to start the ₹750 SIP in WhiteOak Capital. Remember, you'll need to pay your annual income tax return (ITR) within 30-40 days of filing next year to claim tax benefits under this fund. Step 3: Set up the ₹1050 SIP in Bank of India Mid & Small Cap Equity & Debt Fund. This fund comes with mixed equity/debt allocation and so doesn't need special tax return to claim benefits. Ensure this is linked to your bank account. Step 4: Set up the remaining ₹1050 SIPs in Unifi DAA and ₹450 in Axis Gold ETF. Always set these to 'Growth Option' to compound your returns automatically.
review_checkpoints: After 3 months: Check that your SIPs are running and money is debited correctly. Don't worry about returns yet; just make sure the system works. Step 1 is complete when you see the first 3 months' worth of debits. This also confirms you have the emergency fund and are building your portfolio. In this case, we skip emergency fund for now.

After 1 year: Check if your total portfolio value has grown. You should see your portfolio grow by 10-15% (this is normal growth). If the equity side (Stocks + Hybrid) drops more than 20% from your starting value, lower the target corpus slightly and keep investing that extra cash to build dividends. This is a 'bear' scenario. Since you are new, keep investing (SIP) without stopping even if the portfolio dips. This buys more units at lower prices. Over time this works in your favor. Rebalance if your portfolio becomes more than 20% stock-heavy. If that happens, move the excess to the debt portion
plan.rebalancing_guidelines: Once a year, on your birthday, check if the percentages shown in your app match the target split (25% ELSS / 35% Hybrid / 25% DAA / 15% Gold). If one has grown way bigger than its target (like if Hybrid grows to 45% but you want 35%), sell the extra amount from that fund and put it into the fund that is below target. This keeps your risk balanced and prevents you from having too much exposure to just one asset class. Since you have a new regime tax bracket, you cannot claim 80C tax benefits, but we still use an ELSS fund for the specific reason it holds value well and helps build your emergency fund during initial years.
plan.rationale: You are very young (22 years old), giving you the advantage of time. Even with a conservative mindset, you can afford to take calculated risks now because you have 38 years to recover. Since the new regime is tough on tax savings, we still use the ELSS fund because it doubles as a tax haven and tax-efficient wealth builder. We avoided regular debt funds because they are taxed at your high income-tax rate (30%), cutting returns in half. Instead, we use smart hybrid and dynamic funds that are taxed more favourably. The mix gives you a floor if things go wrong and a roof if they go well.
risks: Markets can drop 20-30% in a bad year. The stock-heavy parts of your portfolio will suffer, but the gold and debt parts will help. Over your 38-year timeline, no single downturn will be the end of your journey.
Taxation in the new regime has no 80C benefits, but we still have the tax-saving fund because of its other merits.
ELSS funds are locked for 3 years. If money is needed earlier, you might get a loss (but you won't need it), so only invest what you can leave untouched.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

sip_step_up.description: Increase SIP by 10% every year to match salary growth, boosting wealth faster.

aps.reasoning:
passive_instrument_fraction: 0.0 — The equity portion of the portfolio is entirely allocated to actively managed funds: WhiteOak ELSS (active tax-saving fund), Bank of India Mid & Small Cap Equity & Debt (actively managed hybrid), and Unifi Dynamic Asset Allocation (active asset allocation). There are no index funds or ETFs in the equity sleeve. Axis Gold ETF is passive, but gold is excluded from this dimension's scope.

turnover_score: 0.7 — Rebalancing is recommended annually (on birthday) or if allocations deviate by more than 20% from target. This is relatively infrequent, leaning passive, but the plan includes dynamic asset allocation via Unifi DAA, which implies active internal trading. The review after 1 year includes potential rebalancing, but no short-term tactical shifts are advised.

cost_emphasis_score: 0.6 — The plan explicitly recommends 'Direct Plans' to save on fees and mentions that they are cheaper than Regular Plans. This shows cost awareness, but cost is not the primary selection criterion; fund performance, tax efficiency, and manager track record are emphasized more.

research_vs_cost_score: 0.0 — The rationale focuses on manager track record ('WhiteOak has a great track record', 'manager has a fantastic track record'), fund behavior, and tax implications rather than broad market exposure. There is no mention of index-based or market-cap-weighted logic; instead, deep fund-specific reasoning is used.

time_horizon_alignment_score: 1.0 — The plan is built for a 38-year horizon, with gradual de-risking over time and a retirement trigger at year 38. SIP step-up and long-term compounding are encouraged. The strategy is clearly aligned with long-term wealth creation.

portfolio_activeness_score: 0.0 — All equity-oriented funds are actively managed: ELSS with stock-picking, hybrid fund with active equity-debt mix, and DAA fund with market-timing strategy. Even though risk metrics are missing, the fund categories and rationales indicate high active management. Gold ETF is passive, but it's a small portion and not equity. Debt exposure is embedded in hybrid funds, which are actively managed. No closet indexers or passive instruments dominate the portfolio.

pqs.reasoning: The plan shows strong goal alignment given the investor's long 38-year horizon and conservative risk appetite. It uses hybrid and dynamic allocation funds to moderate risk while maintaining equity exposure for growth, which is appropriate for a young conservative investor. The inclusion of gold (15%) and a dynamic asset allocation fund supports diversification across asset classes, though the fund count is low and mid/small-cap focus in one hybrid fund introduces concentration risk, limiting diversification score. Risk-return is moderately appropriate: while the rationale acknowledges conservatism, a 75% effective equity exposure (via ELSS, hybrid, and DAA) may stretch the bounds of a conservative profile, especially with no emergency fund setup despite it being a stated goal. Internal consistency is weakened by contradictions: the plan states the investor is in the 'new_regime' tax bracket (no 80C benefit), yet recommends an ELSS fund primarily for tax saving and claims tax benefits, which is invalid under the new regime. This undermines coherence. Tax efficiency is poorly addressed: the plan incorrectly assumes ELSS provides tax savings under the new regime, and fails to recognize that debt/liquid funds are slab-taxed. It recommends an ELSS fund without justification based on tax efficiency (since it doesn't apply), and does not consider arbitrage or aggressive hybrid funds that offer equity taxation without lock-in. The rationale claims to avoid debt funds due to high taxation but still includes a hybrid fund with debt, without clarifying the tax treatment difference. Overall, the plan misunderstands key tax rules for the new regime, leading to a low tax_efficiency score.
